title: Increase Database Connection Pool Size
symptom_pattern: Connection pool exhausted, acquisition timeouts, active connections pinned at pool maximum during high load with no connection leak.
steps: Confirm active connections are at the configured pool maximum via database and app metrics.
Verify the database has spare max_connections headroom before increasing the pool.
Raise the application maximumPoolSize incrementally (e.g. 20 -> 40 -> 60).
Set a bounded connection-acquisition timeout (e.g. 5s) so callers fail fast instead of hanging.
Redeploy or hot-reload config and watch pool utilization and p99 latency recover.

title: Recover Leaked Database Connections via Rolling Restart
symptom_pattern: Active connections rise gradually with no traffic spike and are never returned to idle, indicating a connection leak exhausting the pool.
steps: Identify the leaking service by correlating rising idle-in-transaction connections with a specific app.
Perform a rolling restart to reclaim leaked connections without full downtime.
Enable pool leak detection (e.g. leakDetectionThreshold) to surface the offending code path.
Patch the code to release connections in a finally/using block.
Monitor connection counts over several hours to confirm the leak is resolved.

title: Mitigate Memory Leak with Rolling Restart and Cache Bounds
symptom_pattern: Steadily climbing heap or RSS memory, increasing GC pauses, and eventual OOMKilled restart loops without a corresponding traffic increase.
steps: Capture a heap dump or memory profile before restarting for later root-cause analysis.
Perform a rolling restart to relieve immediate memory pressure and stop OOM loops.
Identify the unbounded structure (cache, queue, or listener registry) from the profile.
Add a hard size cap with LRU/TTL eviction or backpressure to bound growth.
Deploy the fix and watch memory plateau over a full traffic cycle.

title: Renew and Deploy an Expired TLS Certificate
symptom_pattern: Sudden TLS handshake failures or certificate-expired errors, often at a clean time boundary, affecting all clients of an endpoint.
steps: Confirm the expiry with openssl s_client / x509 -enddate against the affected host.
Issue a renewed certificate via ACME or the internal CA.
Deploy the new certificate and private key to the ingress or service secret.
Reload the ingress/proxy and verify the served certificate chain and validity dates.
Automate renewal (cert-manager) and add expiry alerting at least 30 days out.

title: Roll Back Configuration to Last Known-Good
symptom_pattern: Behavior or performance regression that correlates with a config or feature-flag change rather than a code deploy; environment config has drifted from baseline.
steps: Diff current config against the last known-good version in source control.
Identify the specific changed key(s) responsible for the regression.
Revert the config or feature flag to the last known-good value.
Apply the change and confirm the affected metric returns to baseline.
Add validation gates and drift detection to prevent recurrence.

title: Fail Over DNS to a Secondary Resolver
symptom_pattern: DNS resolution timeouts or NXDOMAIN for internal/external hostnames, widespread connection errors, resolver health checks failing.
steps: Confirm resolution failures with dig/nslookup against the primary resolver.
Switch resolv.conf or the service mesh DNS config to the secondary resolver.
Flush negative DNS caches across affected hosts.
Verify hostnames now resolve and connectivity is restored.
Add a redundant secondary DNS provider and lower record TTLs where appropriate.

title: Enable Circuit Breaker for a Degraded Upstream
symptom_pattern: A downstream dependency slows or errors, causing request pile-up, thread starvation, and cascading timeouts in the calling service.
steps: Identify the degraded upstream from dependency latency/error dashboards.
Enable or tune a circuit breaker to open on the upstream's error/latency threshold.
Tighten the upstream call timeout so callers fail fast instead of queuing.
Provide a fallback (cached data or graceful degradation) while the breaker is open.
Monitor recovery and let the breaker half-open to test upstream health.

title: Roll Back a Bad Deployment to the Previous Release
symptom_pattern: Error rate or latency spikes immediately after a deploy; the regression correlates directly with a specific release version.
steps: Confirm the regression started at the deploy timestamp of the suspect release.
Halt the rollout/pipeline to prevent further propagation.
Roll back to the previous known-good release via the deployment controller.
If the release included a schema migration, revert it in a backward-compatible way.
Verify error rate and latency return to baseline, then post-mortem the regression.

title: Scale Out Service Replicas to Absorb Load
symptom_pattern: Concurrency or traffic exceeds current capacity (e.g. flash sale), saturating pools, CPU, or throughput on the existing replica count.
steps: Confirm the bottleneck is capacity, not a leak or a single hot dependency.
Increase the replica count (HPA target or manual scale) to spread load.
Ensure downstream databases have connection headroom for the added replicas.
Watch per-instance utilization normalize as new replicas take traffic.
Right-size autoscaling thresholds to handle similar future spikes.

title: Promote a Read Replica to Primary (Database Failover)
symptom_pattern: Primary database is unhealthy or unreachable and cannot be recovered in place; application connection pools cannot acquire working connections.
steps: Confirm the primary is genuinely unrecoverable and replication lag is minimal.
Fence the old primary to prevent split-brain writes.
Promote the healthiest read replica to primary.
Repoint application connection strings/DNS to the new primary and recycle pools.
Verify writes succeed, then rebuild a new replica from the promoted primary.
